content.full_text:
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 19, 1992

Arnold Reddoch, Chairman
Board of Directors
Standish-Litchfield Fire
Protection District
P.O. Box 120
Standish, CA 96128

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-92-336

Dear Chairman Reddoch:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities as Chairman of the Board of
Directors of the Standish-Litchfield Fire Protection District
pursuant to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ogelsby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) In addition, the
Commission's advice is limited to the provisions of the Act.

QUESTIONS

1. May you participate as Chairman of the Board of Directors
for the Standish-Litchfield Fire Protection District when the
board considers payments to your own business for the purchase of
fuel for district vehicles?

2. May you participate as Chairman of the Board of Directors
for the Standish-Litchfield Fire Protection District when the

___________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Our File No. A-92-336
., Page 2


board considers the district's annual budget if a portion of the
budget is set aside to purchase fuel from your business?

CONCLUSIONS

1. You may not participate in the consideration or approval
of payments to your own business for the purchase of fuel.

2. You may not participate in any budget decision which
involves a payment to your business. However, you may participate
in the consideration of other portions of the budget and the
adoption of the final budget if: (1) the decision concerning the
purchase of fuel is segregated from the remainder of the budget;
(2) the decision concerning the purchase of fuel is considered
first, and a final decision is reached; and, (3) votes on other
portions of the budget will not result in a reopening or in any
way affect the decision regarding the purchase of fuel from your
business.

FACTS

You are the Chairman of the Board of Directors for the
Standish-Litchfield Fire Protection District (the "district").
The district is currently considering its annual budget. One
component of the budget is the acquisition of fuel for district
vehicles. The district purchases the fuel from a business that
you and your spouse solely own, the Wayside Inn.² You have become
concerned that you may have a conflict of interest with respect to
budget decisions that may affect your business.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 provides that no public official may make,
participate in making or in any way attempt to use his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official knows or has reason to know the official has a
financial interest. As a member of the Board of Directors of the
Standish-Litchfield Fire Protection District, you are a "public
official" as defined in the Act. (Section 82048.)

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable

2 The Lassen County Counsel's analysis that you enclosed with
your materials stated that the business is jointly owned by you
(Virgil Arnold Reddoch) and your spouse, and another couple. In
my telephone conversation of June 11, 1992, the district's
secretary Mona Ingram clarified that the other couple had sold
their interest, and that you and your spouse were now sole owners.

Our File No. A-92-336
. Page 3


from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a
member of his immediate family or on:

     (a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

                    Section 87103(a).

You are the sole owner of the Wayside Inn, a business entity
in the jurisdiction.  We assume your interest is worth $1,000 or
more.  Consequently, you are required to disqualify yourself from
any decision which could foreseeably³ have a material financial
effect on the Wayside Inn.

     It is clearly foreseeable that the payment or nonpayment of
bills owed to the Wayside Inn will have a financial effect on your
business interest.  However, the foreseeable financial effect on
your business interest must also be material to require
disqualification.

     The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether an effect is material, depending on the specific
circumstances of each decision.  Under the terms of both your
questions, the Wayside Inn is directly before your agency.⁴  Where
a business interest is directly before the board, as an applicant
or the subject of the decision, Regulation 18702.1(a)(2) provides
that the effect of the decision on the business interest is deemed
to be material and disqualification is required.  (Combs Advice
Letter, No. A-89-171.)  Therefore, you must disqualify yourself
from participating in those decisions which involve payments to
the Wayside Inn.

     However, you may be able to participate in discussions of
other aspects of the budget and the adoption of the final budget


_________________________
³  Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.  An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.  Certainty is not required.  However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.  (In
re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

⁴  A business entity is directly before your agency when it
initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal,
or similar request, or is a named party in, or the subject of, the
proceeding.  A business entity is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the business entity.  (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

Our File No. A-92-336
Page 4

plan. In many cases, large and complex decisions, like the budget
decisions, may be divided into separate decisions so that if you
have a disqualifying interest with respect to one component of the
decision, you may still participate in other components.5
(Merkuloff Advice Letter, No. I-90-542.)

Where decisions are separable, you may vote on other
components of the budget if the decision on the other components
will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on any of your economic interests and the following procedure is
complied with:

1. The decision in which you have a disqualifying financial
interest is segregated from the other budgetary decisions;

2. The budgetary decision concerning the Wayside Inn is
considered first, and a final decision reached without your
participation; and

3. Once a final decision has been made on the Wayside Inn,
you may participate in the deliberations and vote regarding other
items in the budget so long as those deliberations will not result
in a reopening or in any way affect the decision from which you
were disqualified.

Finally, you may also vote on the adoption of the final
budget, despite the fact that the provision concerning the
Wayside Inn is included, under the same circumstances discussed
above. (Cook Advice Letter, No. A-83-163.)

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa

_____________________________
5 However, under some circumstances, a series of decisions may be
too interrelated to be considered separately. For example, where
there are two alternatives with respect to a budgeted amount, and
you have a conflict of interest with respect to one of the
alternatives, you may not participate in the consideration of the
other alternative because it is interrelated to the alternative
for which you have a conflict of interest.
